name: After Action Review (AAR)
year_developed: 1970s (formalized in Army doctrine; codified in FM 7-0; major update June 2021; dedicated TC 7-0.1 published February 2025)
progenitor: U.S. Army (Training and Doctrine Command / TRADOC); formalized through FM 7-0 and ADP 7-0; dedicated Training Circular TC 7-0.1 published by TMD in 2025
driving_insight: The AAR's driving insight is that soldiers and units already possess the knowledge of what went right and wrong during an operation, but that knowledge remains tacit, fragmented, and uncalibrated until it is surfaced through structured, facilitated self-discovery. The facilitator does not lecture or grade; they ask neutral questions that force participants to compare intended outcomes against actual outcomes and articulate the delta themselves. This transforms a debrief from a top-down critique (which triggers defensiveness) into a bottom-up learning event (which triggers ownership). The AAR operationalizes the principle that the best learning happens when practitioners reconstruct their own decision-making under the guidance of a neutral party who asks 'what was supposed to happen? what actually happened? what caused the difference? what will we sustain and what will we improve?' rather than telling them what went wrong.
decision_style: deliberative and consensus-oriented. The AAR is not a voting mechanism or a command decision forum; it is a facilitated professional discussion where participants collectively reconstruct what happened and converge on shared lessons. The facilitator guides but does not dictate findings. Decisions about what to sustain and improve emerge from group consensus, though the commander retains authority to make final determinations about retraining priorities and SOP changes. The AAR's power comes from the fact that participants discover problems themselves rather than being told about them by superiors, which creates psychological ownership of the lessons learned.
problem_class_targeted: The AAR targets performance improvement in well-defined operational/training contexts where objectives were specified in advance and outcomes can be observed and compared. It is designed for problems where: (a) there was a clear plan or standard against which to measure performance, (b) multiple participants have partial knowledge of what happened that must be synthesized, (c) the root causes of performance gaps are not immediately obvious but can be uncovered through structured discussion, and (d) the goal is organizational learning and future performance improvement rather than assigning blame. It handles cooperative team environments better than adversarial settings. The AAR is most effective for recurring or trainable tasks where lessons can be applied to the next iteration, less so for one-off novel situations where there is no 'next time' to improve toward.
ordered_phases: Four sequential phases per FM 7-0 (2021) and TC 7-0.1 (2025): (1) PLAN — select Observer Controller/Trainers, identify critical training events to observe, schedule stopping points, determine attendees, choose training aids (maps, video, terrain models); (2) PREPARE — review commander's guidance, Training & Evaluation Outlines, and OPORDs; collect observations during the event using a structured observation plan; organize notes by warfighting function or chronological order; rehearse the facilitation; (3) EXECUTE — open with introduction and ground rules (no rank in the room, focus on learning not blame, everyone contributes); then systematically discuss: what was supposed to happen, what actually happened (the delta), why the delta occurred (root causes), what to sustain (strengths), and what to improve (weaknesses); (4) ASSESS — follow-up actions: identify retraining requirements, revise SOPs, integrate lessons learned into unit knowledge management, and forward significant lessons to the Center for Army Lessons Learned (CALL) for enterprise-wide dissemination.
feedback_loops: The AAR contains explicit feedback loops at multiple levels. Within a single AAR session, the discussion of 'what actually happened' often reveals gaps in the 'what was supposed to happen' understanding, causing the facilitator to loop back and clarify the plan/intent before proceeding. At the organizational level, lessons learned from AARs feed into CALL, which disseminates them across the Army, creating an enterprise-wide learning loop. At the unit level, AAR findings directly feed into Training Meetings where commanders adjust future training plans. The Assess phase creates a formal loop: if retraining is required, a new training event is planned with its own AAR, creating a continuous improvement spiral. TC 7-0.1 (2025) strengthens these loops by introducing data-driven AAR tools that enable trend analysis across multiple iterations.
gates: No formal approval gates exist between AAR phases — the process is designed to be lightweight and adaptable. However, implicit quality gates exist: (1) The Plan phase requires the commander or senior trainer to approve the AAR plan (which events to cover, which facilitators to use, time allocation); (2) During Execute, the facilitator must ensure the group doesn't skip from 'what happened' to 'what to improve' without first establishing the root cause delta; (3) In the Assess phase, the commander gates which lessons are forwarded to CALL versus kept at unit level. When the AAR is used in combat operations (versus training), an additional implicit gate exists: operational security review before lessons are disseminated beyond the unit. If a gate blocks, the facilitator or commander either extends the discussion, brings in additional SMEs, or defers the topic to a follow-on AAR.
exit_criteria: An AAR is 'done' when: (1) all critical events identified in the Plan phase have been discussed; (2) participants have reached shared understanding of the delta between intended and actual outcomes for each critical event; (3) root causes for significant deltas have been identified (not necessarily resolved — the AAR may identify causes that require further investigation); (4) sustainment and improvement actions have been documented with owners and timelines; and (5) the commander or senior trainer is satisfied that the key lessons have been captured. Partial completion states are common — an AAR may conclude some topics definitively while flagging others for deeper investigation in a subsequent session. For informal AARs conducted immediately after action, 'done' is simpler: the leader feels the team has identified the key takeaway and can apply it to the next iteration.
decomposition_method: The AAR decomposes performance analysis using three non-mutually-exclusive methods prescribed by FM 7-0. (1) Chronological decomposition: the operation is broken into a timeline of key events, and each event is discussed in sequence — what was the plan for this phase, what actually happened, why, and what to do about it. (2) Warfighting function decomposition: performance is decomposed into the six warfighting functions (Movement & Maneuver, Intelligence, Fires, Sustainment, Command & Control, Protection), and each function is assessed independently. (3) Key event / theme decomposition: the facilitator identifies the 3-5 most critical training events tied to objectives and focuses discussion on those high-leverage moments. The choice of method depends on the type of operation: chronological works best for sequential operations (e.g., a raid), warfighting function works best for combined-arms operations where multiple functions were active simultaneously, and key event works best when time is limited and only the most important moments can be covered.
granularity_guidance: The AAR decomposes down to the level of individual critical events, decisions, or performance episodes that materially affected the outcome. The stopping condition is pragmatic: stop when further decomposition would not yield actionable lessons. FM 7-0 advises facilitators to focus on events where there was a clear standard or objective, the outcome deviated meaningfully from that standard, and the cause of the deviation is not already understood. For training, the Training & Evaluation Outline (T&EO) provides the granularity benchmark — each evaluated task is a natural leaf node for discussion. For combat operations, granularity is driven by the commander's priorities: events that affected mission accomplishment or force protection get deeper decomposition than peripheral events.
boundary_rules: The AAR enforces clean boundaries through three rules. (1) Scope rule: only discuss events within the defined scope of the exercise or operation — don't expand to organizational issues beyond the commander's authority. (2) Role rule: participants speak only to what they personally observed or decided; speculation about events outside their direct experience is redirected to those who were present. (3) Ownership rule: each improvement action is assigned to a single owner with a deadline; no action is left without clear ownership. The boundaries between discussion topics are maintained by the facilitator, who prevents 'scope creep' into adjacent topics before the current one is resolved. The IS/IS-NOT discipline is also implicitly applied: the AAR focuses on what actually happened, not on hypotheticals or counterfactuals, keeping the discussion grounded in observable facts.
risk_assessment_mechanism: The AAR does not have a formalized risk-scoring matrix. Risk assessment is qualitative and discussion-driven: during the Execute phase, the facilitator probes 'what were the risks you identified at the time?' and 'what risks did you not see coming?' This surfaces both recognized risks (those the team planned for) and emergent risks (those that surprised them). The safety risk assessment is handled separately through the Army's Composite Risk Management (CRM) process, which is integrated into the planning phase of training events but not directly into the AAR discussion. TC 7-0.1 (2025) introduces a more structured approach to risk discussion by recommending that facilitators specifically ask about risk decisions made during execution: 'What risk did you accept in that moment? Was the residual risk acceptable given the mission?' This represents an evolution from training-focused AARs to combat-applicable AARs where risk trade-offs are central to learning.
unknown_handling: When the AAR encounters something unknown (e.g., the group cannot determine why something happened, or there is conflicting testimony about what happened), the facilitator follows a structured escalation path. First, probe deeper: ask clarifying questions to see if additional data exists within the group. Second, flag for further investigation: document the unknown in the AAR notes as a 'data gap' requiring follow-up (e.g., review AAR recordings, interview absent participants, consult sensor data). Third, if the unknown relates to enemy actions, task the S2 (intelligence) section to investigate. Fourth, if the unknown cannot be resolved at unit level, escalate to higher headquarters or CALL. The AAR does not halt on unknowns; it documents them, assigns ownership for resolution, and moves on to the next topic. This is a deliberate design choice: the AAR prioritizes forward momentum and broad coverage over exhaustive resolution of every ambiguity.
default_stance_on_unknown: PERMISSIVE (with documentation). The AAR continues forward even when unknowns exist, recording them as open items rather than blocking progress. The facilitator's default is to proceed through the agenda rather than stall on unresolved questions. This is appropriate for the AAR's purpose as a learning event rather than a legal investigation — it is better to surface 80% of lessons with some ambiguity than to stall at the first unknown and miss the remaining insights. The open items list serves as the accountability mechanism ensuring unknowns are not forgotten.
safety_guardrails: In its military context, the AAR has no direct technical safety guardrails (it is a human discussion process, not an automated system). Its safety mechanisms are procedural and cultural: (1) The facilitator enforces psychological safety through ground rules (no rank, no blame, confidentiality within the session); (2) Facilitators are trained (via the Army's Observer Controller/Trainer Academy) to prevent the AAR from becoming a witch hunt or personal attack; (3) For combat operations, operational security review gates lesson dissemination; (4) TC 7-0.1 (2025) formalizes facilitator certification and quality standards. In an AI/agent context, an AAR-like process would need additional guardrails: the AI facilitator must be constrained from surfacing sensitive tactical details to unauthorized parties, must avoid reinforcing cognitive biases (e.g., hindsight bias), and must be designed to detect and flag when agent self-reports are contradictory (a form of integrity checking).
human_decision_points: In the military AAR, humans are fundamentally IN the loop at every phase. Key human decision points: (1) Plan phase — commander decides which events are critical and which facilitators are assigned; (2) Prepare phase — facilitators decide what to observe and how to organize their notes; (3) Execute phase — facilitator decides when to probe deeper, when to move on, and when to redirect the discussion; participants decide what to share and how to characterize their own performance; (4) Assess phase — commander decides which findings warrant retraining, SOP revision, or CALL submission. In an LLM-agent context, the key design question is whether human review is needed for: (a) the AAR plan (which critical events to review), (b) agent self-assessments (which may be self-serving or blind to certain failures), and (c) the final lesson compilation (to ensure organizational priorities are reflected). The Thunderforge program's 'human-on-the-loop' model suggests a pattern: AI generates the draft AAR analysis, human commander reviews and approves before dissemination.
override_capability: The commander can override, redirect, or halt the AAR at any point. Granularity is full-session: a commander can stop the AAR entirely (e.g., if operational security is at risk), can redirect the focus to a different set of events mid-discussion, or can overrule the facilitator's characterization of what happened. Per-subtask override is less common but possible: a commander can interject during the discussion of a specific event to provide direct correction or guidance. In practice, effective commanders avoid overusing this authority because it undermines the self-discovery dynamic — the AAR only works if participants feel safe to speak candidly without fear the commander will immediately override their account. Doctrine teaches commanders to let the facilitator run the AAR and to speak last, if at all, during the discussion phase.
fallback_when_no_human: In the military context, an AAR without human facilitation is generally considered degraded but still possible — units sometimes conduct leaderless AARs using a structured worksheet or checklist. In an AI-agent context, the fallback design would be: if no human is available to review AI-generated AAR outputs, the system should (a) auto-publish tactical/performance lessons to the agent team's shared memory, (b) hold operational/strategic lessons in a queue for human review before dissemination, and (c) apply a confidence threshold — lessons with high cross-agent agreement (e.g., 4+ agents independently identified the same issue) can be auto-applied to SOPs, while lessons with low agreement or high-stakes implications require human approval. The timeout-and-default approach is most appropriate: after a configurable waiting period, auto-apply low-risk improvements and escalate high-risk ones.
max_problem_size: The AAR has been demonstrated at scales ranging from a 4-person fire team debrief (15 minutes) to multi-day brigade-level formal AARs covering complex combined-arms exercises involving thousands of personnel. The 2025 JPMRC (Joint Pacific Multinational Readiness Center) data assessment tools push this further, enabling data-driven AARs across brigade-level exercises with continuous sensor feeds. The theoretical limit is constrained by discussion bandwidth: an AAR requires all key participants to be in the same facilitated discussion (physically or virtually), which becomes unwieldy above division level (10,000+ personnel). Above that threshold, AARs cascade: a corps-level AAR focuses on corps staff decisions, while subordinate brigade AARs handle tactical-level lessons. What breaks at scale: (a) the 'no rank in the room' ground rule becomes harder to enforce as the rank gradient increases, (b) junior participants self-censor, and (c) the sheer volume of observations overwhelms the facilitator's ability to guide productive discussion.
parallel_execution: AARs are naturally parallelizable at the organizational level — each unit can conduct its own AAR simultaneously for its own part of the operation. Within a single AAR session, parallelism is limited: the facilitated discussion is inherently serial (one topic at a time). However, the Prepare phase supports parallelism: multiple OC/Ts observe different parts of the operation simultaneously and compile their observations independently. Post-2025 data-driven AARs enable a form of analytical parallelism where automated tools process sensor data (position tracking, weapons effects, communication logs) in parallel while the human facilitator prepares the discussion flow. In an agent context, the AAR pattern could be parallelized by having multiple AI agents independently analyze different dimensions of the same operation (one agent for logistics, one for fires, one for maneuver) and then fusing their analyses into a consolidated AAR briefing.
team_scaling_model: Sub-linear scaling with diminishing returns beyond brigade level. Within a small unit (squad to company), adding more participants enriches the discussion by bringing more perspectives. Beyond company level (~150 participants), the discussion becomes dominated by senior voices, junior participants contribute less, and the facilitator struggles to maintain engagement. The doctrinal solution is hierarchical cascading: a battalion AAR focuses on battalion-level decisions with company commanders participating, then each company conducts its own AAR with platoon leaders, cascading down. This hierarchical model produces linear or slightly sub-linear scaling of total lessons captured relative to organization size, since each cascade level captures unit-specific lessons that would be lost in a single large AAR.
cost_scaling_model: The primary cost is time: facilitator preparation, session duration, and participant time away from other duties. For formal AARs, preparation-to-execution ratio is roughly 2:1 or 3:1 (2-3 hours of preparation per hour of AAR discussion). Session duration scales roughly linearly with the number of critical events covered, with 4-8 events being typical for a half-day formal AAR. Informal AARs have near-zero prep cost and 15-30 minute execution cost. The computational cost in a traditional military context is negligible (human-driven). In an LLM-agent context, the cost would scale roughly linearly with the number of agents whose traces must be analyzed, multiplied by the context window required to represent each agent's decision history. The parallelism described above can offset this, making the total cost sub-linear in practice.
replanning_built_in: Replanning is not a first-class phase of the AAR itself — the AAR is a retrospective assessment, not a planning process. However, the AAR is designed as a direct feeder into replanning: its outputs (sustainment actions, improvement actions, lessons learned) are explicitly intended to modify future plans and SOPs. In the broader Army Operations Process (ADP 5-0), the AAR sits within the Assess activity, which directly feeds back into Plan and Prepare. The replanning is local rather than global: an AAR identifies specific improvements to specific procedures, not a wholesale redesign of the operation. The FRAGO (Fragmentary Order) mechanism is the doctrinal vehicle for turning AAR insights into replanning during ongoing operations.
trigger_mechanism: AARs are triggered by the completion of a training event or operation, not by failure. The Army doctrine emphasizes that AARs should be conducted after successes as rigorously as after failures — the trigger is event completion, not performance quality. Re-triggering (a follow-on AAR) may occur if: (1) retraining reveals new issues not surfaced in the initial AAR, (2) an investigation uncovers information that changes the understanding of what happened, or (3) a similar event in the future reveals that previously identified lessons were not effectively institutionalized (triggering a meta-AAR about the learning system itself). In combat, triggers also include: significant friendly casualties, unexpected enemy tactics, or major deviations from the plan.
adaptation_cost: The AAR itself has low adaptation cost — it is designed to be flexible. Informal AARs can be conducted with zero preparation. The cost of adapting training plans based on AAR findings varies: a simple 'sustain this, tweak that' costs little; a finding that requires retraining an entire battalion on a new TTP costs significant training time and resources. The AAR process incurs no lost work cost since it is retrospective: it analyzes completed work, it doesn't interrupt ongoing work. In an agent context, running AARs on completed agent executions is cheap (post-hoc log analysis); the adaptation cost is incurred when the lessons are applied to future agent configurations, which may require prompt engineering changes, tool modifications, or workflow restructuring.
failure_recovery_mechanism: The AAR is itself a failure recovery mechanism — its entire purpose is to detect and recover from performance failures by institutionalizing corrective action. Within the AAR process itself, failure modes include: (1) Facilitator failure — a poor facilitator allows the AAR to become a blame session or a lecture; recovered by the commander stepping in or by using a co-facilitator model. (2) Participant silence — key participants don't speak up; recovered by the facilitator using directed questioning and small-group breakout techniques. (3) Hindsight bias — participants reconstruct events to make outcomes seem more predictable than they were; mitigated by using contemporaneous recordings and data (video, position logs, comms recordings). (4) Lesson decay — lessons are documented but never applied; recovered through the CALL system and training management reviews that audit lesson implementation. In an agent context, blast radius containment would involve: isolating lessons that apply to only specific agent types versus those requiring system-wide changes, and testing changes in sandboxed environments before production rollout.
output_artifacts: The AAR produces: (1) AAR notes/facilitator's guide — structured documentation of what was discussed, organized by warfighting function or chronological order; (2) Sustainment and Improvement Action List — specific actions with assigned owners and due dates; (3) Lessons Learned submission to CALL (if significant) using the standard CALL format; (4) Retraining Plan (if deficiencies require formal retraining); (5) for data-driven AARs (post-2025), structured performance data including position tracks, weapons engagement data, and communication logs with annotations. In an agent context, equivalent artifacts would include: agent execution traces with annotations, an improvement register, and a structured lessons-learned database.
briefing_format: The AAR output is communicated through multiple formats depending on audience. For participants: oral discussion (the AAR itself is the primary communication). For the commander: an AAR summary brief organized around key findings, root causes, and recommended actions. For the institutional Army: the CALL Lessons Learned format, which standardizes the structure (observation, discussion, recommendation, supporting evidence). For training management: entries in the Digital Training Management System (DTMS). The AAR does not use a formal 5-paragraph order format for its own outputs — rather, the OPORD/FRAGO is the mechanism by which AAR-derived improvements are communicated as plan changes.
traceability: Partial traceability. Within a single AAR, the facilitator's notes trace each finding from observation to root cause to recommended action, providing a decision record. Lessons submitted to CALL are archived and searchable, enabling trace-back from institutional knowledge to the original AAR. However, the AAR process does not mandate formal decision logging at the level of individual statements — it relies on the facilitator's notes, which vary in quality. TC 7-0.1 (2025) and the JPMRC data assessment tools improve traceability by adding timestamped, sensor-validated data to supplement human observations, moving toward a more auditable record. In an agent context, full traceability is achievable by design: every agent action has a log, and the AAR analysis can link each finding back to specific log entries.
agent_communication_protocol: Not applicable in the military human context — AAR communication is face-to-face verbal discussion. In an AI-agent implementation, the AAR process would be most naturally implemented as a structured multi-agent protocol: a designated 'AAR facilitator agent' queries execution trace data from participating agents (via MCP or A2A), synthesizes findings, presents them for review, and logs improvement actions to a shared knowledge base. The protocol would be asynchronous (since the AAR happens after execution, not during) and would use structured message formats (trace queries, finding records, action items). Agent discovery would be explicit: the facilitator agent knows which agents participated in the execution and queries them directly.
learning_memory_architecture: The AAR is fundamentally a learning and memory mechanism — it is the primary institutional tool for converting individual and unit experiences into organizational knowledge. At the unit level, AAR lessons are stored in unit SOPs, training plans, and leader notebooks (procedural memory). At the institutional level, CALL serves as the Army's long-term episodic/semantic memory, archiving lessons learned across all units and making them searchable. The Army Training Network (ATN) provides the dissemination layer. The system does not support automated pattern discovery across AARs (that requires human analysts), though JPMRC 2025 tools begin to address this gap. In an agent context, this maps cleanly to: agent execution traces → AAR analysis → lessons stored in a vector database or knowledge graph → retrieval-augmented future planning. Post-hoc fine-tuning or skill distillation from AAR findings would be a natural extension.
latency_bounds: The AAR is designed for interactive (near-real-time) use. Informal AARs occur immediately after action with effectively zero latency. Formal AARs are typically conducted within hours to days of the event, while memory is still fresh. TC 7-0.1 (2025) emphasizes 'AARs during lane training for immediate feedback.' The process degrades gracefully under time pressure: if limited time is available, the facilitator focuses on key events only, uses chronological format (fastest), and defers deep-dive topics to follow-on sessions. An AAR conducted a week after the event is significantly degraded because participant recall decays — this is the primary latency constraint. In an agent context, latency would be measured in seconds to minutes (automated trace analysis and report generation) and would not degrade significantly since agent traces are an immutable record.
evaluation_benchmarking: No standardized quantitative benchmarks exist for AAR quality. Effectiveness is evaluated qualitatively: (1) Did the AAR identify previously unknown performance gaps? (2) Were improvement actions implemented and did they produce measurable performance improvement in subsequent iterations? (3) Did participants report feeling the AAR was valuable versus a waste of time? The JPMRC data assessment tools (2025) introduce the first quantitative metrics: comparing pre-AAR and post-AAR performance on specific T&EO tasks using sensor data. In an agent context, AAR effectiveness could be benchmarked by: (a) fix rate — what percentage of AAR-identified issues are resolved in the next agent execution, (b) recurrence rate — how often do the same issues reappear after being 'learned', (c) false positive rate — how many AAR findings turn out to be spurious on re-examination.
mapping_strength: The AAR maps exceptionally well to LLM-agent workflows. The core structure — compare intended plan vs. actual execution, identify deltas, determine root causes, assign improvements — is directly applicable to agent task execution review. Specific strengths: (1) Agent execution traces provide an objective, timestamped 'what actually happened' record that eliminates the human AAR's primary weakness (faulty human recall and hindsight bias). (2) The facilitator role maps naturally to an LLM with a dedicated system prompt: neutrally compare plan to execution, ask probing questions about deviations, and synthesize findings. (3) The hierarchical AAR cascade (corps → division → brigade → battalion) maps to a hierarchical agent decomposition: a top-level orchestrator agent can conduct an AAR on its own planning decisions while task-level agents conduct AARs on their subtask executions. (4) The 'sustain + improve' output format is directly usable as feedback for prompt refinement, tool configuration, and workflow optimization. (5) The IS/IS-NOT discipline from Kepner-Tregoe can be productively combined with AAR to strengthen root cause analysis.
gaps: Several aspects of the AAR do not transfer directly to agent contexts. (1) Self-discovery assumption: the AAR assumes participants will honestly self-assess when asked neutral questions; LLM agents may produce self-serving or confabulated self-assessments, especially about failures. (2) Tacit knowledge: the AAR relies heavily on surfacing tacit knowledge that participants didn't know they had; LLM agents have no tacit knowledge — everything they 'know' is either in the prompt or the training data. The AAR discussion dynamic of 'aha, now I realize what I was thinking' doesn't have an analog in current LLM architectures. (3) Social dynamics: the AAR's effectiveness depends on group psychological safety and willingness to admit mistakes; multi-agent systems don't have social emotions, but they can exhibit emergent pathologies (e.g., all agents converging on the same wrong answer because they share the same model). (4) Time-boundedness: the AAR is designed for events with clear start/end boundaries; agent workflows may be continuous and ongoing, making it unclear when to trigger an AAR. (5) Counterfactual reasoning: the AAR often explores 'what could we have done differently?' which requires counterfactual reasoning that LLMs are notably weak at.
adaptation_required: To apply the AAR to the Jordan pipeline, several adaptations are needed. (1) Replace human facilitation with a dedicated 'AAR Agent' that has access to execution traces from all pipeline agents and uses a structured analysis protocol (compare plan vs. trace, identify deviations, probe root causes, assign improvement owners). (2) Implement trace-based objective comparison rather than relying on agent self-report: the AAR agent should analyze execution logs, tool call sequences, and intermediate outputs rather than asking agents 'what happened?' (3) Integrate AAR triggers into the pipeline's gating mechanism: after each major pipeline phase (research, plan, execute, synthesize), run a lightweight AAR before passing through the gate. (4) Add a 'lesson application' mechanism: AAR findings should automatically update agent system prompts, tool configurations, and workflow definitions (the 'improvement action' loop). (5) Combine AAR with Kepner-Tregoe IS/IS-NOT for root cause analysis of deviations: when an agent output deviates from expected, use IS/IS-NOT to narrow down whether the cause was in planning, execution, or environment. (6) Store AAR outputs in a structured knowledge base with retrieval augmentation for future planning (learn from past mistakes at retrieval time).
analogous_jordan_node: synthesize (primary), replan (secondary). The AAR's core function is post-execution synthesis — comparing plan to outcome and producing consolidated lessons. It also directly feeds the replan node by providing structured improvement inputs.
validation_context: Extensively validated. The AAR is one of the most battle-tested organizational learning tools in existence, with over 50 years of use across: (1) U.S. Army training — every CTC (Combat Training Center) rotation at NTC, JRTC, and JMRC includes formal AARs as the primary learning mechanism; (2) Combat operations — AARs have been conducted after every major U.S. military operation from Grenada (1983) through Iraq and Afghanistan to present day; (3) Adaptation by other services — U.S. Marine Corps, U.S. Navy, and U.S. Air Force all have AAR equivalents; (4) Civilian adoption — widely used in emergency services (fire, police), healthcare (post-code debriefs), and corporate settings (project post-mortems); (5) JPMRC 2025 provides the most rigorous validation to date, combining human observation with sensor data for objective performance measurement. The AAR's effectiveness is well-documented: units that conduct regular AARs show measurably faster performance improvement than those that don't.
known_failures: Documented failure modes include: (1) Blame-shift AAR — when psychological safety is absent, the AAR becomes a finger-pointing exercise where participants defend themselves rather than learning; common when commanders use AARs to assign fault rather than discover lessons. (2) Ritualistic AAR — the AAR becomes a checkbox exercise where the facilitator goes through the motions but no substantive learning occurs; participants recite expected answers without genuine reflection. (3) Hindsight bias — participants reconstruct events to make outcomes seem inevitable, obscuring the genuine uncertainty that decision-makers faced at the time; this is the most pervasive cognitive failure mode. (4) Lesson decay — lessons are identified but never institutionalized; the same AAR findings recur year after year because the improvement actions were never implemented. (5) Rank gradient effect — junior participants self-censor when senior leaders are present, suppressing the very observations the AAR is designed to surface. (6) Recorder failure — the facilitator fails to capture findings accurately, or notes are lost, and the AAR's value evaporates. (7) Volume overwhelm — in complex operations, the sheer number of observations exceeds the facilitator's capacity to organize productive discussion, leading to superficial coverage. (8) Counterexample: an AAR conducted on a fundamentally flawed plan cannot fix the plan — it can only identify the flaws; the AAR diagnoses, it does not replan.
where_still_used: Active use: (1) All U.S. Army units — mandated by FM 7-0 for all training events; TC 7-0.1 (2025) reinforces this requirement. (2) All U.S. Combat Training Centers (NTC, JRTC, JMRC) — AARs are the centerpiece of the CTC experience. (3) U.S. Marine Corps, Navy, Air Force — service-specific adaptations. (4) NATO allies — widely adopted through interoperability doctrine. (5) Emergency services — fire departments, police tactical teams, EMS agencies use AARs for incident debriefing. (6) Healthcare — surgical teams, emergency departments, and trauma centers use AAR variants for clinical debriefing. (7) Corporate sector — project post-mortems, agile retrospectives, and incident reviews are direct descendants of the AAR. (8) JPMRC (Joint Pacific Multinational Readiness Center) — cutting-edge data-driven AAR implementation. (9) Center for Army Lessons Learned (CALL) — ongoing institutional knowledge management. (10) Open-source: no direct open-source framework named 'AAR', but retrospectives in Agile/Scrum are a recognized descendant.
